originalTitle: Trump seizes on New Jersey election error to boost misleading fraud claims
summary: Republicans have latched on to the announcement after Trump’s lie-laden speech, but experts say DHS’s data is ‘designed to create a false argument’
content: Donald Trump and his allies quickly seized on an announcement from Mikie Sherrill, the New Jersey governor, on Tuesday that 6,600 noncitizens had registered to vote because of a software error, and fewer than 400 of them voted, using the disclosure to escalate false claims that there are scores of noncitizens on the voter rolls. Trump seized on the news days after he said in a primetime speech that the Department of Homeland Security identified 278,000 noncitizens on the voter rolls in New Jersey and three other states. But a Guardian review of the letters the DHS sent the states shows the agency expressed less certainty about the number and has offered no explanation of its methodology. Since 2018, New Jersey has had automatic voter registration, which means that any time someone interacts with a motor vehicle office they are automatically registered to vote, unless they opt out. Between 2023 and 2024, some 6,600 people had indicated they were not citizens, yet nonetheless had been registered to vote. About 400 of those people cast a ballot, Sherrill said. Harmeet Dhillon, the head of the justice department’s civil rights division, sent a letter to Sherrill earlier this week requesting the names of those who were registered and voted and the department is reportedly mulling prosecutions. Sherrill suggested on Thursday the state wasn’t going to turn over the information on voters and said earlier in the week it would be unfair to prosecute them for an error that was the state’s fault. The number represents a small fraction of the more than 6.6 million registered voters in the state. But within hours of the announcement, Trump and other election deniers inflated the number of people who registered to vote because of the error, and held it up as yet another data point that “illegals” are stealing American elections on behalf of Democrats. Trump said in a post on Truth Social: “35,152 is the new number just released by Homeland Security of non-citizens registered to vote in New Jersey. These are just the ones that got caught. The real numbers will prove to be many times this amount. They are all Dumocrats!.” There were Democrats and Republicans erroneously registered, Sherrill said on Tuesday, with the majority unaffiliated with a party. The incident is far from proof of the widespread fraud that Republicans allege, experts say. “It’s not good, I don’t want to suggest otherwise. I don’t want to tell anyone that they’re stupid for thinking it’s less than perfectly optimal. But it’s not really an example of what they’re talking about,” said Stephen Richer, a Republican who oversaw elections in Maricopa county, Arizona, from 2021 to 2025. “These people were trying to do the right thing and acknowledge that they’re not citizens, and then the vast majority of them, even though registered and presumably [were] sent election mail materials, ignored that and didn’t vote.” The number of non-citizens the state says were registered represents a “minuscule fraction” of New Jersey’s active registered voters in 2024, said David Becker, an election administration expert and the executive director of the Center for Election Innovation and Research. “The number of potential ballots cast from those incorrect registrations would represent roughly 0.009% of all ballots cast in New Jersey’s 2024 general election, when Kamala Harris defeated President Trump by more than 250,000 votes. “While administrative errors such as New Jersey’s do infrequently occur, they are very rare and affect a very small fraction of voters. The states entrusted to run our elections are best positioned to detect and correct these errors, as they have access to substantially better data than the federal government,” he said. The firestorm over the New Jersey voter rolls came as Trump escalated his incendiary rhetoric about voting ahead of the midterm elections. Last week, in a primetime address from the White House, he offered what seemed to be an eye-popping statistic: the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) had found 278,000 non-citizens on the rolls in just four states – California, Nevada, Pennsylvania and New Jersey. That, too, is a misleading figure. In its letters to election officials in those four states DHS expressed less certainty about the 278,000 number, saying it was a ceiling of how many noncitizens could be on the rolls. The agency also offered little to no explanation of its methodology for identifying noncitizens on the rolls. It gave the states two weeks to respond. “It’s a laughable letter,” Becker said. “Imagine someone said to you: ‘Some of the items in your fridge are poisonous. Write us back in two weeks and tell us what you’re going to do about that.’” The first question one would ask, Becker said, is “which items?” “They’re not serious. They don’t want the states to even maintain their records correctly. If they did, they would be completely transparent, and they would have acted immediately and said we absolutely need you to do this,” he added. “This is designed to create a false argument post-election that the election was stolen. This has nothing to do with actually cleaning up the lists.” The noncitizen registration claim was part of a slew of misleading information Trump offered in a speech last week designed to weaken confidence in the US election system ahead of November midterm elections in which his party is expected to lose seats. It also comes as Republicans are pushing legislation that would impose new proof-of-citizenship requirements on voter registration, even though several studies have shown noncitizen voting is extremely rare. Last week, both Trump and Markwayne Mullin, the homeland security secretary, cited the 278,000 number as evidence that states should share sensitive voter information with the federal government, with Mullin threatening potential prison time for those who don’t comply. At least one Republican has cited the number as a bona fide example of voter fraud. “We know there ar
chineseTitle: 特朗普利用新泽西州选举错误来助长误导性欺诈指控
chineseSummary: 在特朗普充满谎言的演讲后，共和党人抓住了这一消息，但专家表示，国土安全部的数据“旨在制造虚假论点”
chineseContent: 周二，新泽西州州长米基·谢里尔 (Mikie Sherrill) 宣布，由于软件错误，有 6,600 名非公民登记投票，但其中投票人数不足 400 人，唐纳德·特朗普 (Donald Trump) 及其盟友很快抓住这一消息，利用这一消息进一步强化了选民名册上有数十名非公民的虚假说法。特朗普在黄金时段演讲中表示，国土安全部在新泽西州和其他三个州的选民名册上确认了 278,000 名非公民，几天后他抓住了这一消息。但《卫报》对国土安全部向各州发送的信件的审查显示，该机构对这个数字表示不太确定，并且没有对其方法做出任何解释。自 2018 年以来，新泽西州实行自动选民登记，这意味着只要有人与机动车辆办公室互动，他们就会自动登记投票，除非他们选择退出。 2023 年至 2024 年间，约有 6,600 人表示自己不是公民，但仍然登记投票。谢里尔说，其中大约 400 人参加了投票。司法部民权部门负责人哈米特·迪隆 (Harmeet Dhillon) 本周早些时候致函谢里尔，要求提供登记和投票者的姓名，据报道，司法部正在考虑起诉。谢里尔周四表示，该州不会移交选民信息，并在本周早些时候表示，因该州的错误而起诉他们是不公平的。该数字仅占该州超过 660 万登记选民的一小部分。但在宣布这一消息后的几个小时内，特朗普和其他选举否认者夸大了因这一错误而登记投票的人数，并将其作为另一个数据点来证明这一点。
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
